texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper studies a mixed singular/switching stochastic control problem for a multidimensional diffusion with multiples regimes on a bounded domain. Using probabilistic, partial differential equation (PDE) and penalization techniques, we show that the value function associated with this problem agrees with the solution to a Hamilton-JacobiBellman (HJB) equation. In that way, we see that the regularity of the value function is C 0 , 1 ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc .
texts.text: This paper studies a mixed singular/switching stochastic control problem for a multidimensional diffusion with multiples regimes on a bounded domain. Using probabilistic, partial differential equation (PDE) and penalization techniques, we show that the value function associated with this problem agrees with the solution to a Hamilton-JacobiBellman (HJB) equation. In that way, we see that the regularity of the value function is C 0 , 1 ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords. Singular/switching stochastic control problem, Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations, non-linear partial differential system.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Singular and switching stochastic control problems have been of great research interest in control theory due to their applicability to diverse problems of finance, economy, biology and other fields; see, e.g., [10, 14] and the reference therein. For that reason, new techniques and problems are continuously developed. One of those problems, called mixed singular/switching stochastic control problem, concerns with applying both singular and switching controls in optimal way on some stochastic process that can change regime. Within a regime, a singular control is executed.

texts.orig: Previous to this paper, the problem (1.3) has been studied extensively (both in bounded and unbounded set domains) for separate cases: when the process X ξ,ς cannot change its regime or when the singular control ξ is not executed on X ξ,ς ; see, e.g., [10, 11, 14, 16] and the reference therein. It is natural to study stochastic control problems where both controls are involved, and see how they can be applied.
texts.text: Previous to this paper, the problem (1.3) has been studied extensively (both in bounded and unbounded set domains) for separate cases: when the process X ξ,ς cannot change its regime or when the singular control ξ is not executed on X ξ,ς ; see, e.g., [10, 11, 14, 16] and the reference therein. It is natural to study stochastic control problems where both controls are involved, and see how they can be applied.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, both (singular and switching) controls have been used recently in the study of interactions between dividend and investment policies. In that context, a firm that operates under an uncertain environment and risk constraints wants to determine an optimal control on the dividend and investment policies (see [2, 3, 4, 13]). The authors assumed that the cash reserve process of a firm switches between m -regimes governed by a Brownian motion with the same volatility but different drifts [3, 13], a two-dimensional Brownian motion with the same stochastic volatility but different stochastic drifts [4], or different compound Poisson processes with drifts [2]. The costs and benefits for switching regime are made automatically in the firm's cash reserve and they are not considered in the expected returns.

The authors assumed that the cash reserve process of a firm switches between m -regimes governed by a Brownian motion with the same volatility but different drifts [3, 13], a two-dimensional Brownian motion with the same stochastic volatility but different stochastic drifts [4], or different compound Poisson processes with drifts [2]. The costs and benefits for switching regime are made automatically in the firm's cash reserve and they are not considered in the expected returns.

texts.orig: 1. We characterize the solution u to the HJB equation, which is closely related to the value function V given in (1.3), as limit of a sequence of solutions to another system of variational inequalities that is related to ε -penalized absolutely stochastic continuous/switching control problems; see Subsection 2.2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rest of this document is organized as follows: in Section 2 we consistently formulate the stochastic control problem studied here; see (2.8), and give the assumptions for obtaining the main results of this paper; Theorems 2.3, 2.5. Also we introduce the ε -penalized absolutely continuous/switching control problem and its HJB equation (2.19). Then, in Section 3, a non-linear partial differential system (NPDS) is introduced, where some ` a priori estimates are given. Afterwards, using Lemma 3.2, Proposition 3.3, Arzel` a-Ascoli compactness criterion and the reflexivity of L p loc ( O ); see [6, Section C.8, p. 718] and [1, Thm. 2.46, p. 49] respectively, we prove the existence, regularity and uniqueness of the solution u ε to (2.19); see Subsection 3.1. In Subsection 3.2, Theorem 2.3 is verified whose proof is done by selecting a sub-sequence of { u ε } ε ∈ (0 , 1) . Then, in Section 4, a verification lemma of the ε -penalized absolutely continuous/switching control problem is presented. This lemma is divided into two parts: Lemmas 4.1 and 4.4. Afterwards, we give the proof of Theorem 2.3. In section 5, we draw our conclusions and discuss possible extensions of this paper. Finally, the proofs of Lemma 3.2 and Proposition 3.3 are given in the appendix. To finalize this section, let us say that the notations and definitions of the function spaces that are used in this paper are standard and the reader can find them in [1, 5, 6, 7, 8].

Also we introduce the ε -penalized absolutely continuous/switching control problem and its HJB equation (2.19). Then, in Section 3, a non-linear partial differential system (NPDS) is introduced, where some ` a priori estimates are given. Afterwards, using Lemma 3.2, Proposition 3.3, Arzel` a-Ascoli compactness criterion and the reflexivity of L p loc ( O ); see [6, Section C.8, p. 718] and [1, Thm. 2.46, p. 49] respectively, we prove the existence, regularity and uniqueness of the solution u ε to (2.19); see Subsection 3.1. In Subsection 3.2, Theorem 2.3 is verified whose proof is done by selecting a sub-sequence of { u ε } ε ∈ (0 , 1) . Then, in Section 4, a verification lemma of the ε -penalized absolutely continuous/switching control problem is presented. This lemma is divided into two parts: Lemmas 4.1 and 4.4. Afterwards, we give the proof of Theorem 2.3. In section 5, we draw our conclusions and discuss possible extensions of this paper. Finally, the proofs of Lemma 3.2 and Proposition 3.3 are given in the appendix. To finalize this section, let us say that the notations and definitions of the function spaces that are used in this paper are standard and the reader can find them in [1, 5, 6, 7, 8].

texts.orig: To prove the statements above; Theorems 2.3-2.5, firstly we study an ε -penalized absolutely continuous/switching control problem that is closely related to the value function problem seen previously. At the same time, the solution to the HJB equation related to this stochastic control problem (see Proposition 2.6) helps to guarantee the existence and regularity of the solution to the variational inequalities system (2.9). The verification lemma for this part, which is divided into two lemmas (Lemmas 4.1 and 4.4) will be presented below to provide the proofs of Theorem 2.3 and Proposition 2.6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Under Assumptions (H1)-(H4), the existence and uniqueness for the strong solutions to the HJB equations (2.9) and (2.20) were guaranteed on the space C 0 , 1 ( O ) ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc ( O ). After that, it was proven that the value function V ε for the penalized absolutely continuous/switching control problem seen in Subsection 2.2 satisfies (2.20), showing that for ℓ ∈ I , V ε ℓ ∈ C 0 , 1 ( O ) ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc ( O ). Finally, assuming also that the domain set O is an open convex set and by probabilistic arguments, it was verified that the value function V , given in (2.8), is characterized as a limit of V ε as ε ↓ 0 which permitted to conclude that V satisfies (2.9) and from here, to see that for ℓ ∈ I , V ℓ ∈ C 0 , 1 ( O ) ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc ( O ).
texts.text: Under Assumptions (H1)-(H4), the existence and uniqueness for the strong solutions to the HJB equations (2.9) and (2.20) were guaranteed on the space C 0 , 1 ( O ) ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc ( O ). After that, it was proven that the value function V ε for the penalized absolutely continuous/switching control problem seen in Subsection 2.2 satisfies (2.20), showing that for ℓ ∈ I , V ε ℓ ∈ C 0 , 1 ( O ) ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc ( O ). Finally, assuming also that the domain set O is an open convex set and by probabilistic arguments, it was verified that the value function V , given in (2.8), is characterized as a limit of V ε as ε ↓ 0 which permitted to conclude that V satisfies (2.9) and from here, to see that for ℓ ∈ I , V ℓ ∈ C 0 , 1 ( O ) ∩ W 2 , ∞ loc ( O ).
